subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Assalamu alaykum, young scientist! Welcome to one of the most exciting units in your science journey. In this unit you will learn all about forces. A force is simply a push or a pull. That sounds small, but forces are happening around you every single second of the day, even right now as you read this book.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 3 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit, you will be able to do all of these things. Read them now, and read them again at the end to see how much you have grown.
Explain that a force is a push or a pull that can change how an object moves.
Sort everyday actions into pushes and pulls, and describe their effects.
Tell the difference between contact forces (which need touching, like friction) and non-contact forces (which act at a distance, like magnetism and gravity).
Describe friction as a force that slows movement when two surfaces rub together, and give helpful and unhelpful examples.
Identify which materials are magnetic (iron, nickel, cobalt and steel) and which are non-magnetic.
Explain that magnets have two poles, a north pole and a south pole, and that like poles repel while opposite poles attract.
Understand gravity as a non-contact force that pulls objects toward the Earth, and that weight is measured in Newtons with a forcemeter.
Plan a simple fair test to compare forces, changing only one thing at a time.
Here are the important words for this unit. Do not try to memorise them all at once. You will meet each one again inside the lessons, and they will become easy.

concepts.title: What Is a Force?
concepts.explanation: A force is a push or a pull. That is the whole idea, and it is worth saying again: every force is either a push or a pull. You cannot see a force by itself, but you can always see what a force *does*. When Yusuf pushes open the wooden door of his home, you see the door swing. When Halima pulls the rope of the well, you see the bucket rise. The push and the pull are the forces; the moving door and rising bucket are what the forces do. Forces need two objects. One object gives the force, and another object receives…
concepts.example: Forces can do four main jobs. First, a force can start something moving - a still ball on the grass stays still until a foot pushes it. Second, a force can stop something moving - the goalkeeper's hands stop the flying…

concepts.title: Contact and Non-Contact Forces
concepts.explanation: Scientists sort forces into two families. The first family is contact forces. These forces only work when two objects are touching. When you push a cart, your hands must touch the cart. When friction slows a sliding sandal, the sandal must touch the ground. No touching means no contact force. The second family is non-contact forces. These are the surprising ones, because they work across a gap, without touching at all. There are two you will study in this unit: magnetism and gravity. A magnet can pull a pin toward…
concepts.example: Friction, a push, a pull, air pushing on a kite

concepts.title: Friction: The Force That Slows Things Down
concepts.explanation: Friction is a contact force. Whenever two surfaces rub against each other, friction appears and works *against* the movement, trying to slow it down or stop it. Friction is caused by the tiny bumps and rough spots on every surface. Even a surface that looks smooth is covered in tiny hills too small for your eyes to see. When two surfaces slide past each other, all those little bumps catch and drag. The roughness of the surface changes how much friction there is. Imagine Farah kicking a football. On smooth, packed…
concepts.example: Friction is not our enemy - most of the time it is a great helper. Without friction you could not walk, because your feet would slip like walking on wet soap. Friction between your sandals and the ground is what lets…

concepts.title: Magnets and Magnetism
concepts.explanation: A magnet is a special object that can attract - that means pull toward itself - certain metals without touching them. This is a non-contact force. Hold a magnet a little above a steel paper clip and, when it is close enough, the clip suddenly jumps up and sticks to the magnet. The invisible pull that reaches across the gap is called magnetism. Magnets do not attract everything. This is a very important discovery. Only a few materials are magnetic, meaning a magnet can pull them. The main magnetic materials are…
concepts.example: A magnet is a special object that can attract - that means pull toward itself - certain metals without touching them. This is a non-contact force. Hold a magnet a little above a steel paper clip and, when it is close…

concepts.title: Gravity and Weight
concepts.explanation: Gravity is a non-contact force that pulls everything toward the centre of the Earth. It is the reason a mango falls *down* from the tree and never floats up into the sky. It is the reason water pours down into your cup, the reason rain falls, and the reason you land back on the ground when you jump. Gravity is always pulling, it always pulls downward, and it never switches off. Gravity is invisible and it acts at a distance, so it belongs to the non-contact family, just like magnetism. But unlike a magnet, gravity…
concepts.example: The strength of gravity's pull on an object is called its weight. This is a special science word. Weight is a force, and because it is a force we measure it in Newtons (N), not in the everyday way. A small apple is…

concepts.title: Measuring Forces With a Forcemeter
concepts.explanation: A forcemeter (also called a spring scale) is a tool that measures how strong a force is. Inside it there is a spring. When you hang an object on the hook, gravity pulls the object down, the spring stretches, and a pointer moves along a scale. The bigger the force, the more the spring stretches, and the higher the number the pointer shows. The numbers are marked in Newtons. Reading a forcemeter is like reading a ruler. You look at where the pointer lines up and read the number next to it. Always read at eye level…
concepts.example: Amina hangs four objects on a forcemeter, one at a time, and writes down the weight of each:

explorations.title: Magnetic or Not Magnetic?
explorations.context: To find out which household materials are magnetic and which are non-magnetic.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write what you think: "I predict that a magnet will pull ______ but will not pull ______." Do you think all metals will be magnetic?
explorations.answer: Which objects were magnetic? What are they made of? Were any metals NOT magnetic? Which ones? What do all the magnetic objects have in common?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out which household materials are magnetic and which are non-magnetic.

explorations.title: Friction on Different Surfaces
explorations.context: To find out which surface gives the most friction by seeing how far an object slides.
explorations.prompt: Write your prediction: "I think the ______ surface will have the most friction, so the object will slide the shortest distance on it."
explorations.answer: On which surface did the car travel the furthest? What does that tell you about its friction? On which surface did the car stop the soonest? Why? How is this like a football rolling on smooth ground compared with loose sand?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out which surface gives the most friction by seeing how far an object slides.

explorations.title: Do Magnets Push? Exploring Poles
explorations.context: To discover that magnets can repel (push) as well as attract (pull), depending on the poles.
explorations.prompt: Predict: "When I bring two magnets together, I think they will always ______." Will they always stick?
explorations.answer: When did the magnets pull together? When did they push apart? What is happening when two like poles face each other? Why can you feel a push even though the magnets are not touching?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To discover that magnets can repel (push) as well as attract (pull), depending on the poles.

explorations.title: Which Falls First? Exploring Gravity
explorations.context: To investigate how gravity pulls objects down, and whether heavier things always land first.
explorations.prompt: Predict: "If I drop a heavy object and a light object at the same time, I think the ______ one will land first."
explorations.answer: Which way did every object fall - up, down, or sideways? Did the heavy stone and the crumpled paper land at nearly the same time? Why did the flat sheet fall more slowly? (Hint: think about the air pushing on it.)
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To investigate how gravity pulls objects down, and whether heavier things always land first.

explorations.title: A Fair Test: Does Weight Change Friction?
explorations.context: To plan and run a fair test to see whether a heavier object needs more force to slide.
explorations.prompt: Predict: "I think a heavier box will be ______ to pull, because heavier objects press down harder and make more friction."
explorations.answer: Run the investigation, record your observations, and compare them with your prediction.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To plan and run a fair test to see whether a heavier object needs more force to slide.

methods.title: Magnetic or Not Magnetic?
methods.example: To find out which household materials are magnetic and which are non-magnetic.
steps: Lay all your objects on a table in a row.
Hold the magnet close to the first object and slowly touch it.
Watch: does the object stick to the magnet or not?
Write magnetic if it sticks and non-magnetic if it does not.
Repeat for every object, one at a time.

methods.title: Friction on Different Surfaces
methods.example: To find out which surface gives the most friction by seeing how far an object slides.
steps: Prop up one end of the board on a book to make a gentle ramp. Keep the height the same every time.
Let the toy car go from the top of the bare ramp without pushing it. Only gravity should start it.
Measure how far it travels after the ramp and write it down.
Now cover the ramp with the towel and repeat, releasing from the same spot.
Repeat with each surface. Test each surface three times and use the middle result.

methods.title: Do Magnets Push? Exploring Poles
methods.example: To discover that magnets can repel (push) as well as attract (pull), depending on the poles.
steps: Slide one magnet slowly toward the other on the table.
Notice if they pull together (attract) or push apart (repel).
Now turn one magnet around the other way and try again.
Feel carefully with your fingers: when they repel, can you feel the invisible push?

methods.title: Which Falls First? Exploring Gravity
methods.example: To investigate how gravity pulls objects down, and whether heavier things always land first.
steps: Hold the stone in one hand and the crumpled paper ball in the other, at the same height.
Let go of both at exactly the same moment. Watch which lands first.
Now try the stone and the FLAT sheet of paper, dropped together.
Notice the difference between the crumpled paper and the flat sheet.

methods.title: A Fair Test: Does Weight Change Friction?
methods.example: To plan and run a fair test to see whether a heavier object needs more force to slide.
steps: Loop the rubber band on the box. Pull the box slowly across the floor and watch how much the rubber band stretches - a longer stretch means a bigger pull.
Do this with the empty box first. Note the stretch.
Add a few stones or a book to make it heavier. Pull again on the SAME floor.
Add more weight and pull once more. Change ONLY the weight each time.
Fair Test Plan

workedExamples.title: 1 - Comparing Weights
workedExamples.prompt: Amina hangs four objects on a forcemeter, one at a time, and writes down the weight of each:

workedExamples.title: 2 - Force to Pull Objects
workedExamples.prompt: Omar measures the force needed to pull different objects across the floor with a forcemeter: a plastic toy needs 2 N, a wooden block needs 5 N, a heavy book needs 8 N, and a metal box needs 12 N.
workedExamples.solution: Question: How much more force is needed for the metal box than for the plastic toy? Step 1 - Pick the two numbers. Metal box = 12 N, plastic toy = 2 N. Step 2 - Subtract. 12 N minus 2 N equals 10 N. So the metal box needs 10 Newtons more force. The metal box is heavier and its surface grips the floor more, so friction is greater and you must pull harder. 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor Make up your own forcemeter numbers for three objects. Ask your tutor: "Please give me subtraction questions to compare my objects, then…

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: The unit we use to measure force is the: (a) metre (b) gram (c) Newton (d) second
workedExamples.solution: c - force is measured in Newtons.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: The tool used to measure a force is a: (a) ruler (b) forcemeter (c) thermometer (d) clock
workedExamples.solution: b - a forcemeter measures force using a spring.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: Which force always pulls objects toward the Earth? (a) friction (b) magnetism (c) gravity (d) a push
workedExamples.solution: c - gravity always pulls objects toward the Earth.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Which materials are attracted to a magnet? (a) all metals (b) iron, nickel and cobalt (c) plastic and wood (d) glass and paper
workedExamples.solution: b - only iron, nickel and cobalt (and steel, which is mostly iron) are magnetic.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: Friction happens when: (a) objects float (b) surfaces rub against each other (c) magnets attract (d) objects fall
workedExamples.solution: b - friction happens when two surfaces rub against each other.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: A ball rolls the SHORTEST distance on: (a) smooth concrete (b) loose sand (c) polished floor (d) glass
workedExamples.solution: b - loose sand is rough, gives the most friction, so the ball stops soonest.

practice.prompt: A force is best described as: (a) a colour (b) a push or a pull (c) a kind of metal (d) a sound
practice.answer: b - a force is a push or a pull.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is NOT a force? (a) pushing a door (b) the colour blue (c) gravity pulling down (d) a magnet attracting iron
practice.answer: b - the colour blue is not a force; it does not push or pull anything.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The unit we use to measure force is the: (a) metre (b) gram (c) Newton (d) second
practice.answer: c - force is measured in Newtons.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The tool used to measure a force is a: (a) ruler (b) forcemeter (c) thermometer (d) clock
practice.answer: b - a forcemeter measures force using a spring.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which force always pulls objects toward the Earth? (a) friction (b) magnetism (c) gravity (d) a push
practice.answer: c - gravity always pulls objects toward the Earth.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which materials are attracted to a magnet? (a) all metals (b) iron, nickel and cobalt (c) plastic and wood (d) glass and paper
practice.answer: b - only iron, nickel and cobalt (and steel, which is mostly iron) are magnetic.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Friction happens when: (a) objects float (b) surfaces rub against each other (c) magnets attract (d) objects fall
practice.answer: b - friction happens when two surfaces rub against each other.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A ball rolls the SHORTEST distance on: (a) smooth concrete (b) loose sand (c) polished floor (d) glass
practice.answer: b - loose sand is rough, gives the most friction, so the ball stops soonest.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Two north poles of magnets brought together will: (a) attract (b) repel (c) do nothing (d) melt
practice.answer: b - two like poles (north and north) repel and push apart.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A magnet pulling a pin across a gap is an example of: (a) a contact force (b) friction (c) a non-contact force (d) weight
practice.answer: c - magnetism works across a gap, so it is a non-contact force.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a non-magnetic metal? (a) iron (b) steel (c) copper (d) a steel nail
practice.answer: c - copper is a metal but is non-magnetic.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Weight is: (a) the amount of stuff in an object (b) the force of gravity pulling on an object (c) the colour of an object (d) the size of an object
practice.answer: b - weight is the force of gravity pulling on an object.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Magnetic or Not Magnetic?
activities.materials: Any magnet (a fridge magnet works well); An iron nail, a steel paper clip, a steel spoon; A plastic peg, a wooden spoon, a piece of paper, a glass jar; An aluminium can and a copper coin (if you have them)
steps: Lay all your objects on a table in a row.
Hold the magnet close to the first object and slowly touch it.
Watch: does the object stick to the magnet or not?
Write magnetic if it sticks and non-magnetic if it does not.
Repeat for every object, one at a time.

activities.title: Friction on Different Surfaces
activities.materials: A small toy car or a smooth bottle cap; A flat board, tray, or big book to make a ramp; Different surfaces to cover the ramp: bare board, a towel, a plastic bag, a sheet of sandpaper or rough cloth; A ruler or measuring tape
steps: Prop up one end of the board on a book to make a gentle ramp. Keep the height the same every time.
Let the toy car go from the top of the bare ramp without pushing it. Only gravity should start it.
Measure how far it travels after the ramp and write it down.
Now cover the ramp with the towel and repeat, releasing from the same spot.
Repeat with each surface. Test each surface three times and use the middle result.

activities.title: Do Magnets Push? Exploring Poles
activities.materials: Two magnets (two fridge magnets, or two small bar magnets from an old toy); A smooth table
steps: Slide one magnet slowly toward the other on the table.
Notice if they pull together (attract) or push apart (repel).
Now turn one magnet around the other way and try again.
Feel carefully with your fingers: when they repel, can you feel the invisible push?

activities.title: Which Falls First? Exploring Gravity
activities.materials: A small stone and a crumpled ball of paper (about the same size); A flat sheet of paper (not crumpled); A safe, clear space over soft ground or a mat
steps: Hold the stone in one hand and the crumpled paper ball in the other, at the same height.
Let go of both at exactly the same moment. Watch which lands first.
Now try the stone and the FLAT sheet of paper, dropped together.
Notice the difference between the crumpled paper and the flat sheet.

activities.title: A Fair Test: Does Weight Change Friction?
activities.materials: A small empty box or container; Some stones, sand, or books to add weight; A rubber band (this stretches to show how hard you pull); The same floor for every test
steps: Loop the rubber band on the box. Pull the box slowly across the floor and watch how much the rubber band stretches - a longer stretch means a bigger pull.
Do this with the empty box first. Note the stretch.
Add a few stones or a book to make it heavier. Pull again on the SAME floor.
Add more weight and pull once more. Change ONLY the weight each time.
Fair Test Plan

activities.title: Observe: Measuring Forces With a Forcemeter
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of measuring forces with a forcemeter around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

terms: A push or a pull that can change how an object moves.
A force that moves an object away from you.
A force that brings an object toward you.
A force that only works when objects touch.
A force that works across a gap.
A force that slows motion when surfaces rub.
Attracts iron, nickel and cobalt without touching.
A material a magnet can pull.
A material a magnet cannot pull.
An end of a magnet where the pull is strongest.

vocabulary.meaning: A push or a pull that can change how an object moves.
vocabulary.example: When you pushed your blanket off to get up, that was a force.

vocabulary.meaning: A force that moves an object away from you.
vocabulary.example: Explain that a force is a push or a pull that can change how an object moves.

vocabulary.meaning: A force that brings an object toward you.
vocabulary.example: A magnet can pull certain metals toward it without even touching them.

vocabulary.meaning: A force that only works when objects touch.
vocabulary.example: Understand gravity as a non-contact force that pulls objects toward the Earth, and that weight is measured in Newtons with a forcemeter.

vocabulary.meaning: A force that works across a gap.
vocabulary.example: Understand gravity as a non-contact force that pulls objects toward the Earth, and that weight is measured in Newtons with a forcemeter.

vocabulary.meaning: A force that slows motion when surfaces rub.
vocabulary.example: Understanding friction will help you see why a football rolls further on smooth ground than on sand, why your sandals grip the floor, and why we oil a squeaky door hinge.

vocabulary.meaning: Attracts iron, nickel and cobalt without touching.
vocabulary.example: In this unit you will also meet a very special helper called a magnet.

vocabulary.meaning: A material a magnet can pull.
vocabulary.example: Identify which materials are magnetic (iron, nickel, cobalt and steel) and which are non-magnetic.

vocabulary.meaning: A material a magnet cannot pull.
vocabulary.example: Identify which materials are magnetic (iron, nickel, cobalt and steel) and which are non-magnetic.

vocabulary.meaning: An end of a magnet where the pull is strongest.
vocabulary.example: Explain that magnets have two poles, a north pole and a south pole, and that like poles repel while opposite poles attract.
Explain that magnets have two poles, a north pole and a south pole, and that like poles repel while opposite poles attract.
Explain that magnets have two poles, a north pole and a south pole, and that like poles repel while opposite poles attract.

commonMistakes: Copper and aluminium are metals but a magnet ignores them.
It lets us walk, grip and stop.
They behave differently on the Moon.

realProblems.prompt: Use what you know to answer these real-life questions.
realProblems.answer: Sand is rough and its loose grains grip the wheels, so there is much more friction. Friction works against the movement, so Hassan must push harder to keep the cart going.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Sand is rough and its loose grains grip the wheels, so there is much more friction. Friction works against the movement, so Hassan must push harder to keep the cart going.

realProblems.prompt: Hassan pushes a donkey cart along a smooth road, then reaches a patch of loose sand and has to push much harder. Explain why, using the word friction.
realProblems.answer: The pin jumps up because it is made of iron, which is magnetic. The plastic button stays down because plastic is non-magnetic and a magnet cannot pull it.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The pin jumps up because it is made of iron, which is magnetic. The plastic button stays down because plastic is non-magnetic and a magnet cannot pull it.

realProblems.prompt: Amina drops a pin and a plastic button on the floor. She waves a magnet over them. Which one jumps up and why?
realProblems.answer: The ball rolls further on smooth concrete, because concrete has less friction than dust. Less friction means less force slowing the ball, so it travels further.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The ball rolls further on smooth concrete, because concrete has less friction than dust. Less friction means less force slowing the ball, so it travels further.

realProblems.prompt: A footballer in Mogadishu plays on dust one day and on smooth concrete the next. On which ground will the ball roll further? Explain.
realProblems.answer: A compass is a tiny magnet that lines up with the Earth's magnetism and always points north and south. From north, people can work out the direction of prayer even in an unfamiliar place.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: A compass is a tiny magnet that lines up with the Earth's magnetism and always points north and south. From north, people can work out the direction of prayer even in an unfamiliar place.

realProblems.prompt: Why do many people keep a compass, and how does it help them find the direction of prayer?
realProblems.answer: There is too much friction in the stiff hinge. Idris could add a small drop of oil, which makes the surfaces slippery and reduces the friction so the door opens smoothly.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: There is too much friction in the stiff hinge. Idris could add a small drop of oil, which makes the surfaces slippery and reduces the friction so the door opens smoothly.

realProblems.prompt: Idris finds it hard to open a stiff, squeaky door hinge. Is there too much or too little friction, and what could he do?
realProblems.answer: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What are the two kinds of force? Give the everyday word for each.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The two kinds are pushes and pulls. A push moves something away; a pull brings something toward you.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name two magnetic materials and two non-magnetic materials.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Magnetic: iron and steel (also nickel, cobalt). Non-magnetic: plastic and wood (also glass, copper, aluminium).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain in your own words what friction is.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Friction is a force that appears when two surfaces rub together and works against the movement, slowing it down.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Give one example where friction is helpful.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Helpful friction example: it lets us walk without slipping, or lets bicycle brakes stop the wheel. (Any correct example.)

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between a contact force and a non-contact force?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A contact force needs the objects to touch (like friction); a non-contact force works across a gap (like magnetism or gravity).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What happens when you bring two like poles of magnets together?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Two like poles repel - they push each other away without touching.

questions.question: The unit we use to measure force is the:

questions.question: The tool used to measure a force is a:

questions.question: Which force always pulls objects toward the Earth?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: gravity always pulls objects toward the Earth.

questions.question: Which materials are attracted to a magnet?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: only iron, nickel and cobalt (and steel, which is mostly iron) are magnetic.

questions.question: Friction happens when:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: friction happens when two surfaces rub against each other.

questions.question: A ball rolls the SHORTEST distance on:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: loose sand is rough, gives the most friction, so the ball stops soonest.

questions.question: Two north poles of magnets brought together will:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: two like poles (north and north) repel and push apart.

questions.question: A magnet pulling a pin across a gap is an example of:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: magnetism works across a gap, so it is a non-contact force.

questions.question: Which of these is a non-magnetic metal?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: copper is a metal but is non-magnetic.

questions.question: Weight is:
options: the amount of stuff in an object
the force of gravity pulling on an object
questions.answer: the force of gravity pulling on an object
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: weight is the force of gravity pulling on an object.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit, you will be able to do all of these things. Read them now, and read them again at the end to see how much you have grown.
I can explain that a force is a push or a pull that can change how an object moves.
I can sort everyday actions into pushes and pulls, and describe their effects.
I can tell the difference between contact forces (which need touching, like friction) and non-contact forces (which act at a distance, like magnetism and gravity).
I can describe friction as a force that slows movement when two surfaces rub together, and give helpful and unhelpful examples.
I can identify which materials are magnetic (iron, nickel, cobalt and steel) and which are non-magnetic.
I can explain that magnets have two poles, a north pole and a south pole, and that like poles repel while opposite poles attract.
I can understand gravity as a non-contact force that pulls objects toward the Earth, and that weight is measured in Newtons with a forcemeter.
